text:
We introduce a new characterization of the NP complexity class called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), a natural extension of the span programs defined by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our primary goal is to facilitate the construction of succinct, easily verifiable non-interactive arguments for NP statements. To achieve this, QSPs utilize a novel approach to the arithmetization of Boolean circuits that yields dramatic performance improvements over traditional techniques.

Using QSPs, we construct a Non-Interactive Zero-Knowledge (NIZK) argument in the Common Reference String (CRS) model for Circuit-SAT. Our proof consists of just 7 group elements, while the CRS size and prover computation remain quasi-linear. These properties make the scheme highly practical, a conclusion supported by our implementation; specifically, our NIZK argument achieves the shortest proofs, the most efficient prover, and the fastest verifier of any currently known technique.

We further present a variant of our construction called Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs), which are designed to compute arithmetic circuits over large fields naturally. Finally, we demonstrate how QSPs and QAPs can be applied to the public verification of outsourced computation. In this scenario, a client can verify the results of a function computed by a server in significantly less time than it would take to perform the computation from scratch. The resulting schemes represent the most efficient general-purpose protocols for publicly verifiable computation to date.
